quotes.text: After he had sent her back— Why Zipporah and her two sons returned to Midian, is not certainly known. From the transaction recorded Exodus 4:20, 24 it seems as if she had been alarmed at the danger to which the life of one of her sons had been exposed, and fearing worse evils, left her husband and returned to her father. It is however possible that Moses, foreseeing the troubles to which his wife and children were likely to be exposed had he taken them down to Egypt, sent them back to his father-in-law till it should please God to deliver his people.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: Eliezer This fairly popular biblical name means literally "God is help." Although the birth of this son is hinted at in 4:20, it has not been previously recorded.
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 97-98

notes: Jethro-came with his sons- There are several reasons to induce us to believe that the fact related here is out of its due chronological order, and that Jethro did not come to Moses till the beginning of the second year of the exodus, (see Numbers 10:11) some time after the tabernacle had been erected, and the Hebrew commonwealth established, both in things civil and ecclesiastical. This opinion is founded on the following reasons:1. On this verse, where it is said that Jethro came to Moses while he was encamped at the mount of God. Now it appears, from Exodus 19:1, 2 that they were not yet come to Horeb, the mount of God, and that they did not arrive there till the third month after their departure from Egypt; and the transactions with which this account is connected certainly took place in the second month; see Exodus 16:1. 2. Moses, in Deuteronomy 1:6, 9, 10, 12-15 relates that when they were about to depart from Horeb, which was on the 20th day of the second month of the second year from their leaving Egypt, that he then complained that he was not able to bear the burden alone of the government of a people so numerous; and that it was at that time that he established judges and captains over thousands and hundreds and fifties and tens, which appears to be the very transaction recorded in this place; the measure itself being recommended by Jethro, and done in consequence of his advice. 3. From Numbers 10:11, 29 etc., we find that when the cloud was taken up, and the Israelites were about to depart from Horeb, that Moses addressed Hobab, who is supposed to have been the same as

quotes.text: Jethro, and who then was about to return to Midian, his own country, entreating him to stay with them as a guide while they traveled through the wilderness. It therefore seems necessary that the transaction recorded in this chapter should be inserted Numbers 10. between the 10th and 11th verses. Numbers 10:10-11. 4. It has been remarked, that shortly after they had departed from Sinai the dispute took place between Miriam, Aaron, and Moses, concerning the AEthiopian woman Zipporah whom he had married, (see Numbers 12:1 etc.;) and this is supposed to have taken place shortly after she had been brought back by Jethro. 5. In the discourse between Moses and Jethro, mentioned in this chapter, we find that Moses speaks of the statutes and laws of the Lord as things already revealed and acknowledged, which necessarily implies that these laws had already been given, (Exodus 18:16) which we know did not take place till several months after the transactions mentioned in the preceding chapters. 6. Jethro offers burnt-offerings and sacrifices to God apparently in that way in which they were commanded in the law. Now the law respecting burnt-offerings was not given till after the transactions mentioned here, unless we refer this chapter to a time posterior to that in which it appears in this place. See Clarke's note on "Exodus 18:12". From all these reasons, but particularly from the two first and the two last, it seems most likely that this chapter stands out of its due chronological order, and therefore I have adjusted the chronology in the margin to the time in which, from the reasons above alleged, I suppose these transactions to have taken place; but the matter is not of much importance, and the reader is at liberty to follow the common opinion. As Moses had in the preceding chapter related the war with Amalek and the curse under which they were laid, he may be supposed to have introduced here the account concerning Jethro the Midianite, to show that he was free from that curse, although the Midianites and the Kenites, the family of Jethro, were as one people, dwelling with the Amalekites. See Judges 1:16; 1 Chronicles 2:55; 1 Samuel 15:6. For although the Kenites were some of those people whose lands God had promised to the descendants of Abraham, (see Genesis 15:18, 19) yet, in consideration of Jethro, the relative of Moses, all of them who submitted to the Hebrews were suffered to live in their own country; the rest are supposed to have taken refuge among the Edomites and Amalekites.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: Showing of obeisance consists in bowing down before another person in token of respect, submission, or reverence. Among ancient peoples, with their caste systems, it was an accepted custom. Moses "did obeisance" to his father-in-law (Ex. 18:7), and the sheaves of his brethren "made obeisance" to his sheaf in the dream of Joseph. (Gen. 37:7-9.)
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Mormon Doctrine, 2d ed., p.540

quotes.text: And Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness— Every part of Jethro’s conduct proves him to have been a religious man and a true believer. His thanksgiving to Jehovah (Exodus 18:10) is a striking proof of it; he first blesses God for the preservation of Moses, and next for the deliverance of the people from their bondage.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: Now I know that the Lord is greater than all gods— Some think that Jethro was now converted to the true God; but it is very probable that he enjoyed this blessing before he knew any thing of Moses, for it is not likely that Moses would have entered into an alliance with this family had they been heathens. Jethro no doubt had the true patriarchal religion.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

notes: This ceremonial most likely possessed a juridical function. In the ancient Near East, treaties and pacts were often ratified by the involved parties participating in a solemn meal. This symbolic rite is mentioned

quotes.text: Sacrifices for God— zebachim, slain beasts, as the word generally signifies. We have already seen that sacrifices were instituted by God himself as soon as sin entered into our world; and we see that they were continued and regularly practiced among all the people who had the knowledge of the only true God, from that time until they became a legal establishment. Jethro, who was a priest, (Exodus 2:16) had a right to offer these sacrifices; nor can there be a doubt of his being a worshipper of the true God, for those Kenites, from whom the Rechabites came, were descended from him; 1 Chronicles 2:55. See also Jeremiah 35.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: And Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel to eat bread— The burnt-offering was wholly consumed; every part was considered as the Lord’s portion, and therefore it was entirely burnt up. The other sacrifices mentioned here were such that, after the blood had been poured out before God, the officers and assistants might feed on the flesh. Thus, in ancient times, contracts were made and covenants sealed; See Clarke note on “Genesis 15:13”, etc. It is very likely, therefore, that the sacrifices offered on this occasion, were those on the flesh of which Aaron and the elders of Israel feasted with Jethro.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: Jethro-took a burnt-offering - olah. Though it be true that in the patriarchal times we read of a burnt-offering, (see Genesis 22:2 etc.,) yet we only read of one in the case of Isaac;
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: Jethro, observing the daily routine in the Israelite camp, is highly critical of the inefficient and tiresome procedure employed by Moses in judging the people. The narrative is remarkable in several ways, not least because so important an Israelite institution as the judiciary is ascribed to the initiative and advice of a Midianite priest. This extraordinary fact testifies to the reliability of the tradition and to its antiquity. In light of the hostility that later characterized the relationships between the Midianites and the Israelites, it is hardly likely that anyone would invent such a story. Also remarkable is the secular nature of the judicial agency. Its organizational structure is humanly devised, and its personnel are drawn "from among all the people" (v. zi), from "all Israel" (v. z5)-from the civil and not the ecclesiastical sphere. Elsewhere in the Torah-for instance, in Deuteronomy 17:9 and 19:17-priests and Levites are also involved in the judicial process. Furthermore, it seems that Moses bypasses the existing power structure. The "elders," who usually exercise judicial functions in a tribal-patriarchal society, are, surprisingly, not mentioned. In fact, the tribal divisions are wholly ignored in the appointments to this new judiciary. The restructuring creates a centralized, supratribal system. The judicial machinery itself is decentralized to a certain extent by an internal hierarchy of authorities. Moses, who acts as the supreme judicial authority, functions as the mediator of divine will, but not as lawmaker or as one who dispenses justice by virtue of superior wisdom. The origin of the judiciary in Israel is also recounted in Moses' farewell address. There too it is not attributed to divine command, although no mention is made of Jethro's role. Deuteronomy 1:9-18 tells that Moses found the people too numerous and too litigious for him to bear the judicial burden alone. He therefore suggested that the people select representatives from each of the tribes to share it with him, men who are "wise, discerning, and experienced." The proposal met with popular approval and was promptly put into effect. It involved the same multilevel system as is set forth in Exodus 18:21,25. An added feature is Moses' charge to the judges: "Hear out your fellow men, and decide justly between any man and a fellow Israelite or a stranger. You shall not be partial in judgment: hear out low and high alike. Fear no man, for judgment is God's" (Deut. 1:16-17). Two interesting parallels to these narratives in Exodus and Deuteronomy may be cited. The first comes from Egypt, from the days of Pharaoh Haremhab (ca. 1333-1306 s.c.E.). This pharaoh, who lived not too long before the Exodus, issued a decree for the reformation of the Egyptian judiciary. He writes that he sought out "persons of integrity, good in character," and placed them in the towns of Egypt. He charged them as follows: "Do not enter into close relations with other people, do not accept a gift from another." The other parallel comes from 2 Chronicles 19:5-8, where we are told that King Jehoshaphat (873-849 B.C.E.) appointed judges "in all the fortified towns of Judah, in each and every town," and charged them as follows: "Consider what you are doing, for you judge not on behalf of man, but on behalf of the LORD, and He is with you when you pass judgment. Now let the dread of the Loin be upon you; act with care, for there is no injustice or favoritism or bribe-taking with the Lord our God.
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 99-100

quotes.text: To judge the people— To hear and determine controversies between man and man, and to give them instruction in things appertaining to God.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: From the morning unto the evening.— Moses was obliged to sit all day, and the people were continually coming and going.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: The people come unto me to inquire of God— To know the mind and will of God on the subject of their inquiries. Moses was the mediator between God and the people; and as they believed that all justice and judgment must come from him, therefore they came to Moses to know what God had spoken.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: I do make them know the statutes of God, and his laws.— These words are so very particular that they leave little room for doubt that the law had been given. Such words would scarcely have been used had not the statutes and laws been then in existence. And this is one of the proofs that the transaction mentioned here stands out of its due chronological order; See Clarke’s note on “Exodus 18:5”.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: Thou wilt surely wear away— nabol tibbol, in wearing way, thou wilt wear away — by being thus continually employed, thou wilt soon become finally exhausted. And this people that is with thee; as if he had said, “Many of them are obliged to wait so long for the determination of their suit that their patience must be soon necessarily worn out, as there is no one to hear every cause but thyself.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

notes: I will give thee counsel, and God shall be with thee— Jethro seems to have been a man of great understanding and prudence. His advice to Moses was most appropriate and excellent; and it was probably given under the immediate inspiration of God, for after such sacrificial rites, and public

quotes.text: Jethro volunteers his services as a management consultant. He fills for Moses the role that Joseph had filled for Pharaoh.
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 101

quotes.text: And the work that they must do.— For it was not sufficient that they should know their duty both to God and man, but they must Do it too; yaasun, they must do it diligently, fervently, effectually; for the paragogic nun deepens and extends the meaning of the verb.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: Thou shalt teach them ordinances— chukkim, all such precepts as relate to the ceremonies of religion and political economy. And laws, hattoroth, the instructions relative to the whole system of morality.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: And shalt show them the way— eth hadderech, That very Way, that only way, which God himself has revealed, and in which they should walk in order to please him, and get their souls everlastingly saved.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

quotes.text: Many other revelations also condemn covetousness. "Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's wife; thou shalt not covet thine own property." (D. & C. 19:25-26; 88:123; 136:20.) "Beware of covetousness: for a man's life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth." (Luke 12:15.) In commanding men to repent "of all their covetous desires," the Lord asked, "What is property unto me? saith the Lord." (D. & C. 117:4, 8.) For leaders in his earthly kingdom the Lord seeks men who hate covetousness. (Ex. 18:21.)
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Mormon Doctrine, 2d ed., p.168

quotes.text: Jethro now defines the ideal social, spiritual, and moral qualifications for judgesthose necessary to create and maintain a healthy and just legal order. In the corresponding account in Deuteronomy 1:13 the requirements for judges are given as "wisdom, discernment and experience.
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 101

quotes.text: If thou shalt do this thing, and God command thee— Though the measure was obviously of the utmost importance, and plainly recommended itself by its expediency and necessity; yet Jethro very modestly leaves it to the wisdom of Moses to choose or reject it; and, knowing that in all things his relative was now acting under the immediate direction of God, intimates that no measure can be safely adopted without a positive injunction from God himself. As the counsel was doubtless inspired by the Divine Spirit, we find that it was sanctioned by the same, for Moses acted in every respect according to the advice he had received.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke

notes: he lets the king be the model for the whole nation, in the DAVIDIC COVENANT. The Davidic Covenant was a step away from the Sinaitic Covenant, which was a step down from the Abrahamic Covenant. King David is a model for all who make the covenant and keep it. David was a Priest, a king, and governing in the House of Israel. He was a model of one man who gets into the covenant and keeps it. You have to watch David close because you are watching you. He is a model of the covenant, like the Levites became a model. Watch how many times Moses goes up and down the mountain. Look at the mountain. At the base of the mountain was a ring. There was an altar there, and all the people could go around it and worship. But they weren't allowed to go past that line. If they did, they were shot or stoned. There was a place up the mountain, half way up, where Moses took Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel (Ex.24). There saw, talked and ate with Jehovah. Then Moses left them and went all the way up to the top, and spoke to the Lord face-to-face. There is the model of the temple, the House of God. It is also the model for the construction of the tabernacle. Now you see all the models come together. The same story told over again, in a different context. God is the same, but he administers different according the conditions of the children of men. [narrative BN referring to an overhead - "We are told in D&C 88, there is celestial, terrestrial and telestial space. Jacob's Ladder, he saw the earth. What happens at the earth level. Christ sacrifices. Then you move into terrestrial space, Millennium; then you move into Celestial space. Come to Mount Sinai, the line around the base is telestial space. There is the altar of sacrifice where all the people can worship. Then part way up where you took the sanctified priests, terrestrial space, where they meet with Christ. Then Moses goes up to the top, which represented the Celestial Kingdom. Now look at the Tabernacle. You have the outer court where the altar was, there is the telestial world, where the Lord came to offer his sacrifice. There is also the world/Egypt where they offered sacrifice. Water to wash. Then you come into the Holy Place, where only the Priests could go ... part way up the mountain as it were. You have the wilderness, which is terrestrial space. What did they all center around and camp around? The center place of Zion, the Mountain of the Lord's house. From the Holy Place the priest went into the Holy of Holies, through a veil. Notice on Mount Sinai there was always a cloud, as a veil, and Moses could pass through the cloud to get to the top. Those below couldn't see what was going on. Notice the veils on the handout. Jordan river, veil over the earth, veil in the tabernacle. Even with Abraham, with the carcasses on the ground, you are getting the same story, just told from a different point of view. Remember the three sets of carcasses, each one representing the progressive levels telestial, terrestrial and celestial. What was Jacob's Ladder. Recall it was represented the next day by the stone. He took it and put oil on the top of it. There are two things that are anointed. When the priest was made a priest for the tabernacle, the oil from the tabernacle was used to anoint. His clothing was anointed, he is the anointed one, the M-S-CH, THE MESSIAH, THE ANOINTED ONE. That is what Messiah means. The anointed one. They anointed kings and priests. Those anointed were models of the ultimate Kings and Priests we all hope to be, as well as of the Great King of Kings, and High Priest, Christ. When you are reading in Leviticus, in the tabernacle itself, notice what they did to everything in the tabernacle. They anointed everything: the shewbread table, the light stand, the altar of incense, the Ark of the Covenant, and the mercy seat. Put oil on them, blessed them and sanctified them. So what does the tabernacle become? THE ROCK THAT JACOB ANOINTED. And what was the Rock? The Messiah. Both are models of Christ. What does the stone altar represent? What about the altar of sacrifice, in the outer court? Who is the Stone of Israel? Christ. What is the burnt sacrifice on top of the altar? Christ. What about the water in the font? The pure water of purification. Christ is the water of life. There is the lamp stand in the Holy Place. Who does that represent? Who is the light of the world? Christ The lamp stand also represented anciently the Tree of Life. Look at the table of shewbread. Twelve loaves of bread on the table, the 12 tribes of Israel. Who is the bread of life? Christ! There is the altar of incense. Smoke going up into heaven, representing the prayers of Israel rising up to God. Who is the intercessor for man with God? Christ. There is the veil. Paul says in Hebrews that the veil represents the body of Christ. That is one reason why at his crucifixion it was rent

City of New Jerusalem. The children of Israel were really good at mummering. Don't be too hard on them. They were hungry. They were out in the desert. They were hot. There was no growing season to grow food. No water to drink. Your whole family is there. Probably any one of us would have mummered also. It is easy to sit here and accuse them, but you are not in their place. We know of the manna that came, the water from the rock. So let's move right to Mt. Sinai. This must be seen in a specific way. Ex.19:1-4 IN the third month, when the children of Israel were gone forth out of the land of Egypt, the same day came they into the wilderness of Sinai. 2 For they were departed from Rephidim, and were come to the desert of Sinai, and had pitched in the wilderness; and there Israel camped before the mount. 3 And Moses went up unto God, and the LORD called unto him out of the mountain, saying, Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel; 4 Ye have seen what I did unto the Egyptians, and how I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself. Where did he bring them? Mt. Sinai, "unto myself." It became known as the House of God, the place where he resides. The temple. In the Lord's own language, he brought them to him, out of Egypt, on eagles' wings. Also see Rev.12, when the church is taken on wings of eagles into the wilderness. Exodus is the pattern for the future, the Restoration, etc. This is why it is important to watch for the Covenant words which are preserved through all the prophets, into latter-day revelation. 5 Now therefore, if ye will obey my voice indeed and keep my covenant, then ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me above all people: for all the earth is mine. That is important. The word peculiar in Hebrew isn't "strange" but it means private property. When the High Priest went into the Holy of Holies to plead, he wore the private property or private jewels of Jehovah, the precious stones representing the house of Israel. And each stone had a name of each house of Israel on it. It was fastened on the shoulders of the High Priest with gold chains, symbolic of Christ entering into the throne of God pleading for the house of Israel. The fact that it was on his chest, rep-resents the closeness of Christ/heart to the House of Israel. His "private treasury, or inheritance." When you enter into the covenant and keep it, you are considered personally his. Peculiar doesn't mean strange, but private treasure. Notice they become his, above all other people. Every one else is considered NOT A PEOPLE, in covenant language. There are only two people on the earth, again...just like Christ and Satan. You have the group of God called MY PEOPLE, who are inside the covenant, and those outside, who are NOT A PEOPLE. MY PEOPLE ARE THOSE WHO HAVE ENTERED INTO THE COVENANT AND KEPT IT; NOT A PEOPLE ARE THOSE WHO HAVE NOT ENTERED INTO THE COVENANT, OR THOSE WHO HAVE BEEN IN IT AND LEFT IT OR BROKEN IT. These are CUT OFF FROM AMONG THE PEOPLE. D&C 121:19 - when the Lord explains the curse upon those who brought Joseph and the saints to the condition they were in in Missouri, the term was very clear. "I WILL SEVER THEM FROM THE ORDINANCES OF MINE HOUSE." The effect of that is to make them NOT A PEOPLE, cut off from MY PEOPLE; IMPOSSIBLE TO ENTER INTO THE PRESENCE OF THE LORD. It simply cannot be done. Therefore, the curse of being cut off is explained in our own D&C...being cut off means you are disinherited; you are not part of God's private treasury, you are not among His People. They are kept out of the group known as MY PEOPLE, and they receive no inheritance from God. That is the worst punishment God will give to them. Exodus 19:5 5 Now therefore, if ye will obey my voice indeed, and keep my covenant, then ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me above all people: for all the earth is mine: 6 And ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation. These are the words which thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel. There are two types of people, aren't there. There is a kingdom of Kings, and also a priesthood. This is the first language that the covenant is to create a holy nation of Priests and Kings. Were they worthy of it? This is the confusing part. No, but because they refuse to live up to their covenants, Jehovah pulls out a couple of them and lets them model the result. He takes the Melchizedek Priesthood out of their midst, leaving them with the Aaronic Priesthood, and those priests model the ultimate model, the great High Priest. And they didn't do so well with that, and wound up with a king, so Jehovah lets them have a king;

side of him were those thieves. Ordinarily, the Romans didn't care how long they hung there, but in this case, the Jews wanted them dead before they started the Sabbath. Because it was the Jews' crucifixion, and they were moving into the Sabbath, they wanted the bodies off the cross before 6:00 p.m. So in this case, the Romans were asked to make sure all three were dead and take them down. That is why they broke the bones off the malefactors on either side, to hasten their death. The soldier came to Jesus, and pierced his side with the spear, completing the type, of no broken bones. They felt that the shock would kill them, and also, with broken bones in their legs they would have no support, and they would suffocate from the pressure on the lungs. In the Passover, they put the blood of the lamb on their doorposts; They roasted the lamb and ate it, representing the purification wrought in us by the Holy Ghost. Nothing of it remained. That represents the infinite and complete sacrifice. Only those of the covenant were allowed to partake of the Passover. So if you weren't circumcised, and part of the covenant, you weren't allowed to eat of the lamb. Notice that the blessings of the Atonement are really only fully given to those who are in the covenant. From the 14th to the 21st day of the month, the time of Passover, in the spring, the Crucifixion of Christ was at the time of the Passover. [BN refers to another over overhead] This one compares Israel in bondage in Egypt, to man in captivity in the telestial world. First, as Israel was in bondage in Egypt, man is in bondage to sin. Prophets are sent to show how to escape. Ordinances, such as Passover, show deliverance from captivity through death of the firstborn. The death of the Savior, the Firstborn, shows us deliverance from sin and bondage. The passage through the Red Sea was baptism. They were guided and protected by the pillar of fire, like we are after receiving the Holy Ghost, as a guide and protector. Don't forget that the fire showed the way through the Red Sea, meaning the baptism of the Holy Ghost. They were sustained by Manna; as we are sustained by the Bread of Life, which is Christ. They were proved and tested through trials in the wilderness, as we are proved and tested in this life. The faithful inherited the promised land, just as the faithful in this life will inherit the Celestial Kingdom. BN draws a picture of Egypt and the Holy Land. This is the model. Of course, it follows the same pattern of Abraham, the sacrifice; the cutting of the carcasses. It follows the same pattern as Jacob's ladder. Follow the pattern. Egypt, the sacrifice of the lamb is the first ordinance; Note in Moses 1, where God tells Moses: 6. And I have a work for thee, Moses, my son; and thou art in the similitude of mine Only Begotten; and mine Only Begotten is and shall be the Savior, for he is full of grace and truth… We also understand from Moses that he was given a vision of creation BEFORE he went into Egypt. In relation to similitudes, when Christ was born, he came out of "eternity." Moses, as a similitude, also comes "out of eternity" having just had a vision of all eternity. He saw all the way to the end of the earth; he saw creation. So he comes from creation, to this world. Doesn't that sound familiar. Moses is the HIGH PRIEST of the FIRST EXODUS. The FIRST ORDINANCE to get them out of Egypt, under their High Priest, is SACRIFICE of the LAMB. The BM explains what that represents. Jesus tells the Nephites in 3 Nephi 9, what it is that he wants. 20 And ye shall offer for a sacrifice unto me a broken heart and a contrite spirit. And whoso cometh unto me with a broken heart and a contrite spirit, him will I baptize with fire and with the Holy Ghost, even as the Lamanites, because of their faith in me at the time of their conversion, were baptized with fire and with the Holy Ghost, and they knew it not. So what is really on the altar under the law of Moses, when they are offering up the Lamb, and the other sacrifices? It is really the person, offering up THEIR WILL to God. It always has represented that. Jesus didn't come and exchange apples for oranges. The sacrifice on the altar has always represented the offering of will, the offering of obedience; the broken heart and contrite spirit. The bulls and rams and goats, and the lamb, were intended to show the broken heart, the contrite spirit. Who made the altar of sacrifice? Christ. Mark 14 clearly shows this principle. At what point did Jesus have the greatest struggle in doing what the Father wanted him to do? In Gethsemane. What was the struggle? The contest of wills.

Whose will would prevail, the Father's or the Son's. If you do my will, I don't want to go through with this. But we will do it according to your will, and not mine. So what is the ultimate sacrifice? The sacrifice of will; that is what we offer on the altar of sacrifice. So when you want to get delivered from the telestial world, you must offer up a sacrifice of will, your broken heart and contrite spirit to your God. That is really the only acceptable sacrifice. If you give God material things, you are only giving him things from the material world which he already created and owns. He knows where all the gold and diamonds are. A few dollars of tithing and fast offering, aren't that critical to him. But what is the principle he is teaching? The free will offering. We think its cute to have our four year old pay tithing on the dime or quarter he received for his chores. It really isn't very much. Maybe the heavens have the same perspective as the angels watch us pay our big tithing of $5,000 a year...when God has the whole universe. The angels probably say, "Isn't that cute." "They're giving him back what he gave them in the first place!" Guess what we have that is ours, that which we own, that he wouldn't take from us? Our AGENCY, our WILL. That is the ultimate sacrifice. We need to keep tithing and fast offering in perspective. Yes, it is a commandment, and that is how the kingdom is built; but what does God really want? Our free will. The next step in extracting one's self from this earth is the baptism; entering into the covenant and agreeing to do some-thing, which Paul taught us in I Cor. 10:1-3, that the Red Sea was the baptism of water and the Holy Ghost. At the same time, they are protected from the Pharaoh by the pillar of fire. It went around behind them. Read the wording in Exodus 14:10-12 - [Remember that Pharaoh got close enough with his army that they could see him coming, and they were very frightened] 10 And when Pharaoh drew nigh, the children of Israel lifted up their eyes, and, behold, the Egyptians marched after them; and they were sore afraid: and the children of Israel cried out unto the LORD. And they said unto Moses, Because there were no graves in Egypt, hast thou taken us away to die in the wilderness? wherefore hast thou dealt thus with us, to carry us forth out of Egypt? Is not this the word that we did tell thee in Egypt, saying, Let us alone, that we may serve the Egyptians? For it had been better for us to serve the Egyptians, than that we should die in the wilderness. NOTICE- "I told you so." Leave us alone. Look at the fine mess you have gotten us into. Notice also Moses' great confidence. 13 And Moses said unto the people, Fear ye not, stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD, which he will shew to you to day: for the Egyptians whom ye have seen to day, ye shall see them again no more for ever. So what does he say? Take a good look, because you will never see them again, ever. Now, watch what the angel of God do? He moves and goes behind them. The pillar goes from before them, to behind them. 19 And the angel of God, which went before the camp of Israel, removed and went behind them; and the pillar of the cloud went from before their face, and stood behind them: 20 And it came between the camp of the Egyptians and the camp of Israel; and it was a cloud and darkness to them, but it gave light by night to these: so that the one came not near the other all the night So the pillar is behind them, and it divides in two. Pharaoh gets lost in the darkness and can't get to the Israelites. The same cloud that was light to Israel was darkness to the Egyptians. NOTICE, that at the Second Coming, the very fire that cleanses the earth and removes the wicked is the same fire that saves the righteous and becomes the glory of the Terrestrial earth. Nephi taught that the Lord will save the righteous is if it must needs be by fire. 1 Nephi 22:17 17 Wherefore, he will preserve the righteous by his power, even if it so be that the fulness of his wrath must come, and the righteous be preserved, even unto the destruction of their enemies by fire. Wherefore, the righteous need not fear; for thus saith the prophet, they shall be saved, even if it so be as by fire. This is another type and shadow of the great SECOND EXODUS that will come with the Deliverer, who is Christ. The pillar that saved Israel, that lit the way for them across the sea, is the same pillar that darkened the way for their enemies. You are modeling the SECOND COMING. In fact you are also seeing
